event: Early hominins inhabit the region, evidenced by Paleolithic finds such as the Divje Babe site, home to a bone artifact often interpreted as the world's oldest musical instrument.
Bronze Age pile-dwelling settlements flourish in the Ljubljana Marshes, leaving behind the world's oldest known wooden wheel with an axle.
The Iron Age Hallstatt culture thrives among local communities, producing elaborate situla art and fortified hilltop settlements.
Celtic tribes settle the area, and the kingdom of Noricum gradually forms in the eastern Alps.
Romans establish the colony of Aquileia nearby, beginning Roman influence over the territory of present-day Slovenia.
Rome incorporates the region into the empire, organizing it within the provinces of Noricum and Pannonia.
The Roman city of Emona (modern Ljubljana) is established as a planned colonial town.
Attila's Huns sweep through the region, devastating Roman settlements including Emona.
Slavic ancestors of the Slovenes migrate into the eastern Alps following the Lombard departure for Italy.
Slavic tribes join the realm of Samo, the first known Slavic political union, resisting Avar and Frankish power.
The Slavic principality of Carantania emerges, regarded as an early forerunner of Slovenian statehood.
Carantania accepts Bavarian and Frankish overlordship, accelerating Christianization of the Alpine Slavs.
The Freising Manuscripts, the oldest surviving text in Slovene and the first Slavic text in Latin script, are written.
The Habsburgs acquire Carniola, bringing most Slovene lands under their long-lasting rule.
Ottoman raids begin penetrating Slovene lands, prompting the construction of defensive tabor fortifications over the following century.
A massive peasant revolt sweeps Slovene territory, popularizing the rallying cry 'stara pravda' (old rights).
Protestant reformer Primož Trubar publishes the first printed books in Slovene, founding Slovene literary language.
Jurij Dalmatin publishes the first complete translation of the Bible into Slovene.
Napoleon establishes the Illyrian Provinces with Ljubljana as capital, briefly promoting the Slovene language in administration and schools.
The United Slovenia (Zedinjena Slovenija) program is proclaimed during the revolutions, marking the birth of a modern Slovene national movement.
A devastating earthquake strikes Ljubljana, triggering a major modernization and rebuilding of the city.
Slovene lands join the new State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs, then the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes after the collapse of Austria-Hungary.
The Carinthian plebiscite assigns much of the contested Slovene-populated region to Austria.
The Kingdom is renamed Yugoslavia and reorganized, with Slovene territory forming the Drava Banovina.
Axis powers invade and partition Slovenia among Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy and Hungary, sparking a Partisan resistance movement.
Partisan forces liberate Slovenia, which becomes a constituent republic of socialist Yugoslavia under Tito.
Postwar treaties return much of the Slovene Littoral from Italy, expanding the republic's territory toward the Adriatic.
The Trieste dispute is settled, dividing the Free Territory and granting Slovenia a stretch of Adriatic coastline.
Intellectuals publish Nova revija issue 57, a landmark articulation of Slovene national and democratic aspirations.
Slovenia holds its first multiparty elections and votes overwhelmingly for independence in a December plebiscite.
Slovenia declares independence on 25 June, then prevails in the brief Ten-Day War against the Yugoslav People's Army.
Slovenia gains international recognition and is admitted to the United Nations.
Slovenia joins both NATO and the European Union.
Slovenia adopts the euro, becoming the first new EU member from the 2004 enlargement to do so.
Slovenia becomes the first post-communist state to hold the rotating presidency of the Council of the European Union.
Slovenia weathers a severe banking and financial crisis, narrowly avoiding an international bailout through state recapitalization.
Slovenia and Croatia receive an arbitration ruling on their long-running border and sea-access dispute in the Bay of Piran.
Catastrophic August floods become the worst natural disaster in Slovenia's modern history, prompting a major national recovery effort.
